The **67th annual [Venice International Film Festival](/wiki/Venice_Film_Festival "Venice Film Festival")**, was held from 1 to 11 September 2010, at [Venice Lido](/wiki/Venice_Lido "Venice Lido") in [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy").[[1]](#cite_note-line-up-1)

American filmmaker [Quentin Tarantino](/wiki/Quentin_Tarantino "Quentin Tarantino") was the jury president for the main competition.[[2]](#cite_note-BBC_News-2) The [Golden Lion](/wiki/Golden_Lion "Golden Lion") was awarded to *[Somewhere](/wiki/Somewhere_(film) "Somewhere (film)")* by [Sofia Coppola](/wiki/Sofia_Coppola "Sofia Coppola").[[3]](#cite_note-labiennale_awards-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4)

Chinese-Hong Kong filmmaker [John Woo](/wiki/John_Woo "John Woo") received the [Golden Lion for Lifetime Achievement](/wiki/Golden_Lion_for_Lifetime_Achievement "Golden Lion for Lifetime Achievement") during the festival.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

In a break with tradition of limiting a film to receiving no more than one major award, the [Special Jury Prize](/wiki/Special_Jury_Prize_(Venice_Film_Festival) "Special Jury Prize (Venice Film Festival)") and the [Volpi Cup for Best Actor](/wiki/Volpi_Cup_for_Best_Actor "Volpi Cup for Best Actor") ([Vincent Gallo](/wiki/Vincent_Gallo "Vincent Gallo")) went to the same film, [Jerzy Skolimowski](/wiki/Jerzy_Skolimowski "Jerzy Skolimowski")'s *[Essential Killing](/wiki/Essential_Killing "Essential Killing")*. In the past, no one film had been given two major awards. Representing the jury, American director [Quentin Tarantino](/wiki/Quentin_Tarantino "Quentin Tarantino") appealed to Festival head [Marco Müller](/w/index.php?title=Marco_M%C3%BCller_(director)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Marco Müller (director) (page does not exist)") to alter the rules. This rule change continues to be upheld for future editions of the Festival.[[6]](#cite_note-Inside_Out_Film-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) Following the Festival, Italian film critic [Paolo Mereghetti](/wiki/Paolo_Mereghetti "Paolo Mereghetti") criticized the decisions that the jury made in awarding prizes, and singled out Tarantino, accusing him of favoritism, which he denied.[[8]](#cite_note-QT_criticism-8)[[9]](#cite_note-guardian.co.uk-9)

The festival opened with *[Black Swan](/wiki/Black_Swan_(film) "Black Swan (film)")* by [Darren Aronofsky](/wiki/Darren_Aronofsky "Darren Aronofsky"), and closed with *[The Tempest](/wiki/The_Tempest_(2010_film) "The Tempest (2010 film)")* by [Julie Taymor](/wiki/Julie_Taymor "Julie Taymor").[[10]](#cite_note-10)

## Official Awards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=67th_Venice_International_Film_Festival&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Official Awards")]

Starting with the 67th edition of the festival, four new awards were established for the *Orizzonti* section: the Orizzonti Award (for feature films), the Special Jury Orizzonti Prize (for feature films), the Orizzonti Award for Short Experimental Films and the Orizzonti Award for Short/Medium-length Films.[[19]](#cite_note-New_awards-19)

The [Golden Lion](/wiki/Golden_Lion "Golden Lion") was won by *[Somewhere](/wiki/Somewhere_(film) "Somewhere (film)")*, directed by [Sofia Coppola](/wiki/Sofia_Coppola "Sofia Coppola"), a film based in part on Coppola's childhood as the daughter of acclaimed American director [Francis Ford Coppola](/wiki/Francis_Ford_Coppola "Francis Ford Coppola").[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4) [Quentin Tarantino](/wiki/Quentin_Tarantino "Quentin Tarantino"), the president of the jury that awarded the prize, hailed the film saying, "it grew and grew in our hearts, in our minds, in our affections".[[26]](#cite_note-26) The jury's decision was unanimous. Upon receiving the award, Coppola paid credit to her father for "teaching me".[[27]](#cite_note-27) The Russian film *[Silent Souls](/wiki/Silent_Souls "Silent Souls")* and the Chilean film *[Post Mortem](/wiki/Post_Mortem_(2010_film) "Post Mortem (2010 film)")* had been considered favorites for the award.[[28]](#cite_note-28)
